drugs.name: Imiquimod 5%
drugs.schedule: three times per week for 4–16 weeks

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with PeIN or Ta disease. Application site reactions may occur. Monitor for adherence and toxicity. Longer, less frequent application may have better response than shorter, more frequent application.
For patients with PeIN or Ta disease. Response rates vary (48% to 74%). Local toxicity and adverse events may occur. Treatment duration may be adjusted based on clinical response.

The use of therapeutic lasers (CO2, Nd:YAG, and KTP) to treat selected (clinical stage PeIN, Ta, and pT1a) primary penile tumors has been reported with acceptable outcomes. Perioperative application of 3%–5% acetic acid can identify HPV-infected skin. A plume evacuator is required. Laser settings vary by type (see Principles of Penile Organ-Sparing Approaches).

Glansectomy may be considered for select patients with distal tumors (clinical stage PeIN, Ta, T1) on the glans or prepuce. For patients with pT1a disease, glansectomy is not recommended unless required to ensure complete tumor eradication with negative margins. Negative surgical margins should be determined from frozen sections. Treatment may be followed with a split- or full-thickness skin graft to create a neoglans. Local recurrence rates are low (2.6% to 16.7%).

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Wide Local Excision with Skin Graft
tier_1_regimens.notes: For early-stage penile cancer confined to the skin with little or no invasion (clinical stage PeIN, Ta, T1). Surgical margins depend on location. Complete excision of the skin with a wide negative margin is needed and may require a split-thickness skin graft (STSG) or full-thickness skin graft (FTSG). If positive surgical margin(s), re-resection should be considered. 2-year recurrence rate may reach up to 50%.

An alternative to wide local excision in select cases. Thin layers of cancerous skin are excised and viewed microscopically until a tissue layer is negative for the tumor. Allows for increased precision, although the success rate declines with higher stage disease. May be preferable for a small superficial lesion on the proximal shaft to avoid total penectomy for an otherwise fairly low-risk lesion.

For primary penile cancer (T1–2, N0) with tumor <4 cm, circumcision followed by brachytherapy alone (category 2B) should be performed with interstitial implant. Brachytherapy should only be performed in centers with significant experience. A 10-year cancer-specific survival of 92% has been reported for tumors confined to the glans.

For primary penile cancer (T1–2, N0) with tumor <4 cm, EBRT alone (category 2B) total dose 65–70 Gy with conventional fractionation using appropriate bolus to primary penile lesion with 2 cm margins. Consider prophylactic EBRT to inguinal lymph nodes in patients who are not surgical candidates or who decline surgical management.

tier_1_regimens.site: Penis (T1–2, N0; tumor ≥4 cm or node-positive, surgically unresectable)
tier_1_regimens.notes: For tumors ≥4 cm or node-positive disease that is surgically unresectable, circumcision followed by EBRT combined with chemotherapy (category 3). Total dose 65–70 Gy with conventional fractionation using appropriate bolus to primary penile lesion with 2 cm margins. For potential radiosensitizing agents, see Principles of Systemic Therapy.

Partial penectomy should be considered the standard for high-grade primary penile tumors, provided that a functional penile stump can be preserved and negative margins are obtained. If a partial penectomy is not possible, a total penectomy should be performed. Intraoperative frozen sections are recommended to determine negative margins.

Total penectomy is necessary when there is invasion into the corpora cavernosum in order to achieve a negative margin, or if partial penectomy is not possible. Intraoperative frozen sections are recommended.

Indicated for patients with intermediate/high-risk features (T1b or any T2 or greater) and clinically negative groins. A modified/superficial inguinal dissection with intraoperative frozen section is an acceptable alternative. If positive lymph nodes are found on frozen section, convert to standard, full-template lymphadenectomy. Consider prophylactic EBRT (category 2B) to inguinal lymph nodes in patients who are not surgical candidates or who decline surgical management.

DSNB is recommended provided the treating physician has experience with this modality. If positive lymph nodes are found on DSNB, ILND is recommended. DSNB should be performed at tertiary care referral centers where at least 20 procedures are done per year. Not recommended in patients with palpable inguinal lymph nodes.

tier_1_regimens.site: Inguinal lymph nodes (palpable non-bulky or bulky)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Ultrasound- or CT-guided biopsy of the most accessible node, inguinal or pelvic. For patients with palpable nodes, this is favored to distinguish reactive lymph nodes from metastatic disease. A negative lymph node biopsy may be confirmed with an excisional biopsy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Inguinal lymph nodes (palpable bulky or enlarged pelvic)

trial_data.trial_name: Pagliaro et al. phase II study
trial_data.outcome: 50% response rate; improved PFS and OS associated with objective response

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to ILND or PLND in patients with ≥4 cm inguinal lymph nodes (fixed or mobile) if FNA is positive for metastatic penile cancer. Patients not eligible for TIP and who are surgical candidates should undergo surgery without neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Repeat every 3 to 4 weeks. Four courses are typically given.

By extrapolation from neoadjuvant data, it is reasonable to give 4 courses of TIP in the adjuvant setting if it was not given preoperatively and the pathology shows high-risk features. High-risk features include pelvic lymph node metastases, extranodal extension, bilateral inguinal lymph nodes involved, and ≥4 cm tumor in lymph nodes.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion on Days 1–4 or Days 2–5

tier_1_regimens.notes: Can be considered as an alternative to TIP in the adjuvant setting. There are no sufficient data to form conclusions about the use of adjuvant chemotherapy. Repeat every 3 to 4 weeks. For more information regarding DPYD testing, see NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

Can be considered for patients with high-risk features (pelvic lymph node metastases, extranodal extension, bilateral inguinal lymph nodes involved, ≥4 cm tumor in lymph nodes). EBRT to 45–50.4 Gy to inguinal and pelvic lymph nodes; boost gross nodes and areas of extracapsular extension to a total dose of 65–70 Gy. Treat primary site of disease if positive margin.

trial_data.trial_name: Pagliaro et al. phase II study
trial_data.outcome: 50% response rate; improved PFS and OS associated with objective response

tier_1_regimens.notes: TIP is a reasonable first-line treatment for patients with metastatic penile cancer, including palliative treatment of patients with distant metastases. Repeat every 3 to 4 weeks. No randomized clinical trials due to rarity of penile cancer.

drugs.name: Fluorouracil
drugs.schedule: continuous infusion on Days 1–4 or Days 2–5

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil has been used historically for metastatic penile cancer and can be considered as an alternative to TIP. It appears to be effective for some patients, although the toxicities may be limiting and may require dose reductions. Repeat every 3 to 4 weeks. For more information regarding DPYD testing, see NCCN Guidelines for Colon Cancer.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: First-line Cisplatin/Fluorouracil + Pembrolizumab followed by Pembrolizumab maintenance

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.schedule: on Day 1 every 3 weeks for 6 cycles

trial_data.outcome: ORR 39.4%; median PFS 5.4 months; median OS 9.6 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Followed by maintenance Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on Day 1 every 3 weeks for up to 34 cycles. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing and administration instructions. Nonrandomized phase II trial.

biomarkers.requirement: tumor must be MSI-H or dMMR

trial_data.outcome: ORR 34.3% (MSI-H/dMMR); median PFS 4.1 months; median OS 23.5 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is preferred for subsequent-line treatment in patients with unresectable or metastatic MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors that have progressed following prior treatment and no satisfactory alternative treatment options. Any tumor-agnostic labels that cover penile cancer can be used based on results of MGPT. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.notes: May be considered in select patients, especially if not previously treated with a similar class of agent. No standard subsequent-line systemic therapy exists. Evidence to support palliative use of second-line therapy is limited.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Penile cancer is a rare malignancy arising from the penis, with squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) accounting for approximately 95% of cases. The penis is an external male genital organ composed of the glans, prepuce (foreskin), and shaft. Embryologically, the penis develops from the genital tubercle. The glans and prepuce are derived from ectodermal tissue, while the corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum are of mesodermal origin. The mucosal surface of the glans and inner prepuce is lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, which is the site of origin for most penile cancers. The disease can present as a visible or palpable lesion on the penis, which may be associated with pain, discharge, bleeding, or foul odor. The lesion may be characterized as nodular, ulcerative, or fungating. Advanced disease may involve palpable inguinal lymph nodes and/or systemic symptoms. Early diagnosis is crucial as penile cancer can lead to disfigurement and significant morbidity. [1,2]

subtypes.name: Verrucous carcinoma
subtypes.description: A low-grade, well-differentiated subtype with low malignant potential that does not metastasize. It includes the variant carcinoma cuniculatum.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Papillary squamous cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: A subtype characterized by a papillary growth pattern.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Warty (condylomatous, bowenoid) carcinoma
subtypes.description: A subtype with a warty, cauliflower-like appearance, often associated with HPV.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.
subtypes.key_associations: HPV-associated; may exhibit a warty or condylomatous pattern. [3,5]

subtypes.name: Basaloid carcinoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive, poorly differentiated subtype with small, basophilic cells arranged in nests or cords.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Clear cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive subtype with cells containing clear cytoplasm.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.

subtypes.name: Sarcomatoid carcinoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive subtype with spindle cell morphology, mimicking sarcoma.
subtypes.frequency: Not specified in the source text.
subtypes.key_associations: Known aggressive histology subtype; high grade. [3,5]

subtypes.name: Squamous cell carcinoma not otherwise specified (NOS)
subtypes.description: Invasive keratinizing carcinoma without special features, for which evaluation of p16 is not available.
subtypes.frequency: Most common type, accounting for the majority of cases.
subtypes.key_associations: Includes usual type (pseudohyperplastic and pseudoglandular variants). [5]

subtypes.name: HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: Subtypes driven by high-risk HPV infection, often with a basaloid or warty morphology.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 45% to 80% of penile cancers are HPV-related.
subtypes.key_associations: Favorable prognostic factor (better disease-specific survival). Associated with HPV types 16, 6, and 18. [4,15,16,17]

subtypes.name: HPV-independent squamous cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: Subtypes not associated with HPV infection, often arising in the setting of chronic inflammation or lichen sclerosus.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 20% to 55% of penile cancers (inverse of HPV-associated).
subtypes.key_associations: Includes usual type and verrucous carcinoma; may have worse prognosis in some subtypes (e.g., sarcomatoid). [4,5]

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular pathogenesis of penile cancer is closely linked to human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, detected in 45% to 80% of cases, particularly HPV types 16, 6, and 18. HPV oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate tumor suppressors p53 and pRb, respectively, leading to genomic instability and carcinogenesis. HPV-positive tumors often show better prognosis, possibly due to immune recognition. In HPV-independent tumors, alterations in the p53 pathway are common, often due to mutations in TP53. Other molecular events include overexpression of EGFR, cyclin D1, and activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. Chromosomal abnormalities such as gains at 8q and losses at 3p, 8p, and 13q have been reported. Multigene panel testing (MGPT) in Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA)-approved laboratories can identify rare and actionable mutations and fusions, which may guide therapy in advanced disease. [6,7,8]

epidemiology.incidence: Squamous cell carcinoma of the penis is rare, representing 0.4% to 0.6% of all male malignancies in the United States and Europe. In 2026, an estimated 2,260 new cases are predicted in the United States. The incidence is higher in developing countries of Asia, Africa, and South America.
epidemiology.mortality: In 2026, an estimated 450 cancer-specific deaths are predicted in the United States. Five-year survival rates vary by stage: localized disease 79%, regional disease 57%, and distant disease 10%.
epidemiology.trends: Not explicitly discussed in the source text.
epidemiology.demographics: The most common age at presentation is between 50 and 70 years, with a median age at diagnosis of 68 years in the United States. The disease is overwhelmingly seen in males. Racial disparities are not explicitly detailed in the provided text, but global variation is noted with higher incidence in low- and middle-income countries. [1,9,10]

risk_factors.detail: Tight foreskin that cannot be retracted over the glans, hindering hygiene and potentially trapping HPV.
risk_factors.strength: Increased risk of penile cancer by 25% to 60%. [4,10,11]

risk_factors.detail: Infection with high-risk HPV types, especially 16, 6, and 18, is strongly associated with penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN) and SCC.
risk_factors.strength: Found in 45% to 80% of penile cancers. HPV or p16 positivity is a favorable prognostic factor. [4,12,13,14,15,16,17]

risk_factors.detail: Increases the risk of HPV infection and associated disease due to immunosuppression.
risk_factors.strength: HIV-positive individuals have a higher risk of HPV acquisition and lower HPV clearance. [18,19]

risk_factors.detail: Smoking cigarettes is a significant risk factor.
risk_factors.strength: Makes individuals 3 to 4.5 times more likely to develop penile cancer. [12,22]

risk_factors.detail: A chronic inflammatory skin condition affecting the genital area.
risk_factors.strength: Associated with a 2% to 9% risk of developing penile carcinoma. [23,24,25]

risk_factors.detail: Inflammation of the glans penis, often due to poor hygiene or infection.
risk_factors.strength: Listed as a risk factor; strength of association not quantified. [4]

risk_factors.detail: Being uncircumcised at birth increases risk, likely due to phimosis and HPV retention.
risk_factors.strength: Neonatal circumcision is associated with a lower rate of penile cancer. [4,14,20]

risk_factors.detail: Inadequate genital hygiene may contribute to smegma accumulation and chronic irritation.
risk_factors.strength: Listed as a risk factor; strength of association not quantified. [4]

risk_factors.detail: History of sexually transmitted infections, particularly HPV and HIV.
risk_factors.strength: Listed as a risk factor; strength of association not quantified. [4]

risk_factors.detail: History of injury to the penis.
risk_factors.strength: Listed as a risk factor; strength of association not quantified. [4]

risk_factors.factor: Psoriasis treatment with psoralen plus ultraviolet A (PUVA)
risk_factors.detail: Patients with psoriasis undergoing PUVA therapy have markedly increased risk.
risk_factors.strength: Incidence increased 286-fold compared to the general population. [26]

protective_factors: Neonatal circumcision: Associated with a lower rate of penile cancer, likely by eliminating phimosis and reducing HPV infection. The protective effect is not seen in adults who undergo circumcision later in life. [1,14,20]

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Penile squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) most commonly presents as a palpable, visible lesion on the penis. The lesion may be associated with pain, discharge, bleeding, or a foul odor, particularly if the patient has delayed seeking medical treatment. Morphologically, the lesion can be characterized as nodular, ulcerative, or fungating. A critical aspect of the presentation is that the lesion may be obscured by phimosis, which can hinder both visual inspection and physical examination. Patients may present with signs of more advanced disease, including palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy and/or constitutional symptoms such as fatigue and weight loss. The primary lesion is most frequently located on the glans (34.5%), prepuce (13.2%), or shaft (5.3%).

symptoms.name: Palpable/visible penile lesion
symptoms.detail: A palpable or visible lesion on the penis, which may be nodular, ulcerative, or fungating.

symptoms.name: Penile pain
symptoms.detail: Pain may be associated with the lesion, especially with more advanced or complicated disease.

symptoms.name: Penile discharge or bleeding
symptoms.detail: Discharge (serous, purulent, or foul-smelling) or bleeding from the lesion.
symptoms.frequency: Common, particularly if ulcerated or infected.

symptoms.name: Foul odor
symptoms.detail: May occur from tissue necrosis or secondary infection, especially with delayed presentation.
symptoms.frequency: Less common, indicates advanced local disease.

symptoms.name: Palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Enlarged, palpable lymph nodes in the groin region.
symptoms.frequency: Present at diagnosis in a subset of patients; indicates regional spread.

symptoms.name: Constitutional symptoms
symptoms.frequency: May indicate advanced or metastatic disease.

signs.name: Phimosis
signs.detail: Tight foreskin that cannot be retracted over the glans. It is a significant risk factor and can obscure visualization of the primary tumor, delaying diagnosis.
signs.frequency: Present in 25% to 60% of penile cancer patients.

signs.name: Penile mass or ulceration
signs.detail: Direct visualization and palpation of the primary tumor on the penile shaft, glans, or prepuce.

signs.name: Inguinal lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable enlargement of inguinal lymph nodes. Note: 30% to 50% of palpable nodes are inflammatory, not metastatic.
signs.frequency: Present at diagnosis in a significant subset.

signs.name: Edema of penis/scrotum/legs
signs.detail: May occur with advanced disease causing lymphatic obstruction.
signs.frequency: Less common, indicates advanced local/regional disease.

atypical_or_missed: Lesion obscured by phimosis: The tight foreskin can prevent visualization and proper examination of the glans and inner prepuce, delaying diagnosis.
Presentation as a chronic, non-healing balanitis: May be misdiagnosed as a benign inflammatory condition.
Painless nodular lesion: May not prompt urgent medical evaluation.
Verrucous carcinoma: Can present as a slow-growing, exophytic, warty mass that may be mistaken for a benign wart or papilloma.

red_flags: Palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy (firm, fixed, or bulky nodes suggestive of metastasis).
Fixed inguinal nodal mass or pelvic lymphadenopathy (cN3 disease).
Constitutional symptoms (unexplained weight loss, significant fatigue).
Invasion into adjacent structures (scrotum, pubic bone) suggesting T4 disease.
Urethral involvement causing obstruction or urinary symptoms.
Bleeding or discharge from the lesion that is persistent or severe.
A lesion that is growing rapidly or has changed in character.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential initial evaluation to characterize risk factors, lesion morphology, and assess for regional lymphadenopathy.
diagnostic.key_findings: Documentation of lesion diameter, location, number, morphology (papillary, nodular, ulcerous, flat), relationship to structures (submucosal, corpora, urethra). Assessment of inguinal nodes for palpability, size, number, mobility, and fixation.

diagnostic.rationale: Required for definitive histologic diagnosis and risk stratification. Provides information on grade, subtype, and prognostic features.
diagnostic.key_findings: Confirmation of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) or penile intraepithelial neoplasia (PeIN). Histologic grade (WHO/ISUP 3-tier: grade 1 well differentiated to grade 3 poorly differentiated). Presence of lymphovascular invasion (LVI), perineural invasion (PNI), depth of invasion.

diagnostic.rationale: HPV status is assessed via morphology, p16 immunohistochemistry, or molecular methods. HPV/p16 positivity is associated with a better prognosis (higher disease-specific survival).
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of HPV-associated subtypes (basaloid, warty). p16 overexpression serves as a surrogate marker for high-risk HPV infection.

diagnostic.rationale: HIV infection is a risk factor for penile cancer and affects management and counseling.
diagnostic.key_findings: Positive serology indicates increased risk and potential need for screening of sexual partners.

staging.rationale: For initial staging workup, to assess for regional lymph node involvement and distant metastases.

To evaluate inguinal and pelvic lymph nodes, as well as visceral organs. MRI is noted as potentially best for enhancing physical examination in patients with difficult-to-assess inguinal regions (e.g., obesity, prior surgery).
staging.indication: Initial staging and assessment of clinically suspicious nodes.
Initial staging and part of surveillance for node-positive patients.

staging.rationale: Has high pooled sensitivity for detecting inguinal lymph node metastasis in patients with clinically node-positive (cN+) disease. Low sensitivity in cN0 disease. Used to evaluate extent of metastatic burden after other imaging shows concern.
staging.indication: Consider in patients with suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease for staging and treatment response assessment.

staging.rationale: Can be used to assess inguinal lymph nodes, especially if physical examination is difficult (obesity, prior surgery).
staging.indication: Surveillance if abnormal clinical exam or difficult inguinal assessment.

staging.test: Percutaneous Lymph Node Biopsy (Ultrasound or CT-guided)
staging.rationale: Minimally invasive method to obtain tissue diagnosis from palpable or radiographically suspicious lymph nodes.
staging.indication: For palpable non-bulky (<4 cm) or bulky (≥4 cm) inguinal nodes, and enlarged pelvic nodes if technically feasible.

biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis, from the primary tumor biopsy.

biomarkers.rationale: To identify rare and actionable mutations and fusions in the metastatic setting. May guide use of tumor-agnostic therapies (e.g., for MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H).
biomarkers.when: Consider in patients with metastatic disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended in metastatic disease to identify potential targeted therapies.

do_not_miss: Histologic diagnosis via biopsy is paramount before any treatment.
Assessment of inguinal lymph node status (physical exam and imaging) is critical, as nodal involvement is the most important prognostic factor.
Imaging of chest, abdomen, and pelvis for staging in invasive disease.
avoid: Blindly treating a penile lesion without a histologic diagnosis.
Relying solely on physical exam for inguinal node assessment; imaging adds critical information.
Bleomycin-containing regimens for systemic therapy due to unacceptable toxicity.
Assuming all palpable inguinal nodes are metastatic; 30-50% are inflammatory.

staging.system: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017) TNM System

t_stages.description: Tumor invades lamina propria (glans), dermis/lamina propria/dartos fascia (foreskin), or connective tissue between epidermis and corpora (shaft), with or without lymphovascular invasion (LVI) or perineural invasion (PNI), and is or is not high grade.
Tumor is without LVI or PNI and is not high grade (i.e., not grade 3 or sarcomatoid).
Tumor exhibits LVI and/or PNI or is high grade (i.e., grade 3 or sarcomatoid).
Tumor invades into corpus spongiosum (either glans or ventral shaft) with or without urethral invasion.
Tumor invades into corpora cavernosum (including tunica albuginea) with or without urethral invasion.
Tumor invades into adjacent structures (i.e., scrotum, prostate, pubic bone).

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
No palpable or visibly enlarged inguinal lymph nodes.
Palpable mobile unilateral inguinal lymph node.
Palpable mobile ≥2 unilateral inguinal nodes OR bilateral inguinal lymph nodes.
Palpable fixed inguinal nodal mass OR pelvic lymphadenopathy unilateral or bilateral.
Lymph node metastasis cannot be established.
≤2 unilateral inguinal metastases, no extranodal extension (ENE).
≥3 unilateral inguinal metastases OR bilateral metastases.
ENE of lymph node metastases OR pelvic lymph node metastases, no ENE.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades lamina propria/dermis/connective tissue without LVI/PNI and not high grade. No nodal or distant metastasis.

stage_groups.criteria: T1b, N0, M0 OR T2, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: T1b: Tumor with LVI/PNI or high grade. T2: Tumor invades corpus spongiosum. No nodal or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified separately; part of localized group.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invades corpora cavernosum. No nodal or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified separately; part of localized group.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Primary tumor T1 to T3 with clinically or pathologically positive unilateral mobile inguinal node(s) ≤2 without ENE. No distant metastasis.
Primary tumor T1 to T3 with multiple unilateral or bilateral positive inguinal nodes. No distant metastasis.

stage_groups.criteria: T4, Any N, M0 OR Any T, N3, M0 OR Any T, Any N, M1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: T4: Tumor invades adjacent structures. N3: Fixed inguinal node or pelvic node metastasis. M1: Distant metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative (with intent to prolong survival and control symptoms).

staging_pearls: The AJCC 8th edition introduced a distinction between clinical (cN) and pathologic (pN) staging for lymph nodes.
pN1 requires ≤2 unilateral inguinal metastases WITHOUT extranodal extension (ENE). pN3 includes any ENE or pelvic node metastasis.
Stage II disease was split into Stage IIA (T1b or T2, N0) and Stage IIB (T3, N0) in the 8th edition.
Lymph node involvement is the single most important prognostic indicator for survival.
Bilateral inguinal node dissection is generally recommended for intermediate/high-risk clinically negative nodes because laterality cannot be predicted.
Crossover lymphatic drainage from left to right and vice versa occurs.

management_principles.overview: The management of penile squamous cell carcinoma is guided by a multidisciplinary approach, prioritizing organ-sparing techniques for early-stage disease to preserve penile function and quality of life, while ensuring oncologic control through surgery, radiation, and systemic therapies as indicated. Treatment philosophy emphasizes accurate staging, risk stratification, and tailored therapy based on tumor stage, histologic grade, lymphovascular invasion, and lymph node status. Curative intent is standard for localized and regional disease, with palliative care integrated for advanced or metastatic settings. The rarity of penile cancer necessitates referral to specialized centers with experience in this malignancy to optimize outcomes [MS-4, MS-19].

by_intent.patient_group: Localized disease (PeIN, Ta, T1a, T1b, T2 or greater, N0-M0)
by_intent.approach: Organ-sparing surgery (e.g., wide local excision, laser therapy, glansectomy) for low-risk lesions; partial or total penectomy for higher-risk or invasive tumors; dynamic sentinel node biopsy or inguinal lymph node dissection for nodal staging; radiation therapy as an alternative for penile preservation in select cases; adjuvant therapies (e.g., chemotherapy, chemoradiation) for high-risk features post-surgery.

by_intent.patient_group: Metastatic or recurrent disease (M1 or unresectable regional disease)
by_intent.approach: Systemic chemotherapy (e.g., TIP, cisplatin/fluorouracil with pembrolizumab) as first-line; radiation therapy for local control or symptom relief; clinical trials for subsequent-line options; best supportive care for refractory disease.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Effective management of penile cancer requires a multidisciplinary team (MDT) including urologic surgeons, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and reconstructive surgeons. The NCCN guidelines are developed by a panel of experts from these disciplines, highlighting the importance of collaborative decision-making. Referral to high-volume centers is recommended to ensure access to specialized techniques such as dynamic sentinel node biopsy and organ-sparing surgeries [MS-1, MS-10].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status, often assessed using the Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) scale, is a critical factor in determining eligibility for systemic therapy and aggressive treatments. Patients with ECOG performance status ≥1 are considered to have poor prognosis and may have limited tolerance for intensive regimens like TIP chemotherapy. In the metastatic setting, performance status helps guide the choice between curative-intent consolidation surgery or palliative systemic therapy and supportive care [MS-3, MS-17].

management_pathways.title: Management of Penile Intraepithelial Neoplasia (PeIN) or Ta Disease

nodes.criteria: Clinical diagnosis of PeIN or Ta (noninvasive)

options.label: Imiquimod 5% or fluorouracil 5%
options.recommendation: For PeIN or Ta, topical imiquimod applied at night three times per week for 4–16 weeks, or fluorouracil cream applied twice daily for 2–6 weeks [PN-A1].

options.label: Wide local excision or circumcision
options.recommendation: Wide local excision with negative margins; circumcision alone may suffice for distal prepuce tumors [PN-A2].

options.label: CO2, Nd:YAG, or KTP laser
options.recommendation: For selected PeIN, Ta, or pT1a lesions; perioperative 3%–5% acetic acid application to identify HPV-infected areas [PN-A1].

options.label: Complete glansectomy
options.recommendation: For distal tumors on glans or prepuce; not recommended for pT1a unless needed for negative margins [PN-A2].

options.label: Mohs micrographic surgery
options.recommendation: Alternative to wide local excision in select cases; thin layers excised microscopically until negative for tumor [PN-A2].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Not typically required for noninvasive disease; surveillance is standard.

management_pathways.title: Management of pT1a Primary Penile Cancer

nodes.criteria: Pathologic stage pT1a (invasion into lamina propria without lymphovascular/perineural invasion, not high grade)

options.label: Wide local excision
options.recommendation: With skin grafting if needed; careful consideration for organ preservation if patient can adhere to close follow-up [PN-2].

options.label: Glansectomy
options.recommendation: Only if required to ensure complete tumor eradication with negative margins [PN-A2].

options.label: Partial penectomy
options.recommendation: When tumor location or size precludes conservative surgery; intraoperative frozen sections for margins [PN-B].

options.label: Brachytherapy or EBRT
options.recommendation: For penile preservation; circumcision first to prevent radiation complications [PN-C].

options.label: CO2 or Nd:YAG laser
options.recommendation: For selected cases with acceptable outcomes [PN-A1].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy generally not indicated; follow-up with surveillance.

management_pathways.title: Management of pT1b or T2 or Greater Primary Penile Cancer

nodes.criteria: Pathologic stage pT1b (lymphovascular/perineural invasion or high grade) or ≥T2 (invasion into corpus spongiosum or cavernosum)

options.label: Partial penectomy
options.recommendation: If functional stump can be preserved with negative margins; intraoperative frozen sections [PN-B].

options.label: Total penectomy
options.recommendation: For extensive invasion into corpora cavernosum or when negative margins cannot be achieved with partial penectomy [PN-B].

options.label: EBRT with or without chemotherapy
options.recommendation: For tumors <4 cm: brachytherapy alone or EBRT; for tumors ≥4 cm or node-positive: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy (category 3) after circumcision [PN-C].

options.label: Chemoradiation
options.recommendation: EBRT 65–70 Gy with concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy for select cases [PN-C].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiation may be considered for high-risk features such as positive margins or nodal involvement.

management_pathways.title: Management of Non-Palpable Inguinal Lymph Nodes
branching_basis: Risk stratification based on primary lesion

nodes.criteria: Non-palpable inguinal lymph nodes with low-risk primary (PeIN, Ta, T1a)

options.label: Active surveillance per schedule
options.recommendation: Clinical examination every 6 months for years 1–2, then annually [PN-3, PN-8].

nodes.criteria: Non-palpable inguinal lymph nodes with intermediate/high-risk primary (T1b or ≥T2, any grade)

options.label: Bilateral inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND)
options.recommendation: Standard or modified ILND for staging; a modified/superficial dissection with intraoperative frozen section is an alternative [PN-3].

options.label: Dynamic sentinel node biopsy (DSNB)
options.recommendation: Recommended only in centers with experience; if positive, proceed to ILND [PN-3, MS-10].

options.label: Prophylactic EBRT
options.recommendation: For patients not surgical candidates or who decline surgery [PN-3].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy not standard for clinically negative nodes.

management_pathways.title: Management of Palpable Non-Bulky Inguinal Lymph Nodes (<4 cm)

nodes.criteria: Unilateral palpable lymph node(s) <4 cm (mobile) with low-risk primary

options.label: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy
options.recommendation: Biopsy of most accessible node; if negative, consider excisional biopsy or surveillance [PN-4].

options.label: Bilateral ILND
options.recommendation: For confirmed metastatic disease; consider neoadjuvant TIP if high-risk features [PN-4].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiation may be considered for pN2–3 disease or extranodal extension.

nodes.criteria: Unilateral palpable lymph node(s) <4 cm (mobile) with high-risk primary

options.label: Bilateral ILND
options.recommendation: Avoid delay from biopsy; proceed directly to ILND; consider neoadjuvant TIP for bulky disease [PN-4].

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP
options.recommendation: Paclitaxel, ifosfamide, cisplatin prior to surgery if disease is resectable [PN-4, PN-D].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy based on pathologic findings post-ILND.

management_pathways.title: Management of Palpable Bulky Inguinal Lymph Nodes (≥4 cm or fixed)

nodes.criteria: Unilateral lymph nodes ≥4 cm (mobile) or <4 cm (fixed), or bilateral palpable nodes

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy
options.recommendation: Four courses of TIP followed by consolidative surgery if response; phase II response rate 50% [PN-5, PN-D].

options.label: ILND and PLND
options.recommendation: Standard or modified ILND with consider PLND; neoadjuvant TIP preferred if feasible [PN-5].

options.label: RT or chemoradiation
options.recommendation: For patients not surgical candidates; EBRT to inguinal and pelvic nodes [PN-5, PN-C].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy (if not given neoadjuvantly) and/or radiation for pN2–3 disease or extranodal extension.

management_pathways.title: Management of Enlarged Pelvic Lymph Nodes

nodes.criteria: Pelvic lymph nodes enlarged on imaging (CT or MRI)

options.label: Percutaneous biopsy
options.recommendation: Biopsy of most accessible node; if not feasible, use PET/CT [PN-7].

options.label: Neoadjuvant TIP
options.recommendation: Followed by imaging to assess response; consolidation surgery if stable or responsive disease [PN-7, PN-D].

options.label: Chemoradiation
options.recommendation: EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy [PN-7].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT or chemoradiation for pN2–3 or extranodal extension post-surgery.

options.label: Treat according to recurrence stage
options.recommendation: Follow primary treatment pathways (PN-1, PN-2) [PN-9].

nodes.criteria: Inguinal recurrence with no prior ILND or RT

options.label: Percutaneous biopsy
options.recommendation: Biopsy of most accessible node; if positive, proceed to ILND or systemic therapy [PN-9].

options.label: ILND
options.recommendation: With or without adjuvant therapy based on pathology [PN-9].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant chemotherapy or chemoradiation for pN2–3 disease.

nodes.criteria: Inguinal recurrence with prior ILND or RT

options.label: Chemotherapy
options.recommendation: TIP or cisplatin/fluorouracil; consider consolidation surgery if response [PN-9].

options.label: RT or chemoradiation
options.recommendation: For local control [PN-9].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment based on response to systemic therapy.

nodes.criteria: Metastatic disease (M1) or unresectable regional disease

options.label: First-line chemotherapy
options.recommendation: TIP (preferred), cisplatin/fluorouracil, or cisplatin/fluorouracil or carboplatin/fluorouracil plus pembrolizumab followed by maintenance [PN-10, PN-D].

options.label: Consolidation surgery
options.recommendation: Bilateral superficial and deep ILND and unilateral/bilateral PLND if disease responds or is stable [PN-10].

options.label: RT for local control
options.recommendation: For symptomatic metastases or local control [PN-10, PN-C].

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy not applicable; focus on systemic treatment and palliation.

nodes.criteria: Recurrent or refractory after first-line therapy

options.label: Subsequent-line therapy
options.recommendation: Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H tumors; paclitaxel or cetuximab in certain circumstances [PN-D].

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Per NCCN Guidelines for Palliative Care [PN-10].

adjuvant_logic.summary: No standard adjuvant therapy; focus on clinical trials and palliation.

items.text: History and physical examination including risk factors (balanitis, chronic inflammation, penile trauma, lack of neonatal circumcision, tobacco use, lichen sclerosus, poor hygiene, sexually transmitted diseases like HIV)
Lesion characteristics: diameter, location, number, morphology (papillary, nodular, ulcerous, flat), relationship to structures (submucosal, corpora spongiosa, cavernosa, urethra)
Punch, excisional, or incisional biopsy for histologic diagnosis
Evaluation of histologic subtype, grade, depth of invasion, perineural invasion, lymphovascular invasion, resection margin status
HPV testing via morphology, p16 immunohistochemistry, or molecular methods
Imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging: CT or MRI of abdomen/pelvis, chest x-ray or CT
CT/MRI of pelvis for nodal evaluation if difficult to assess clinically
Consider FDG-PET/CT for patients with suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease
Assessment for comorbidities affecting treatment eligibility
Multidisciplinary team consultation including urology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, radiology
Consider molecular testing (MGPT) for metastatic disease to identify actionable mutations

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary curative treatment for penile cancer, aiming to achieve negative margins while preserving penile function when feasible. It includes organ-sparing approaches for early-stage disease and more extensive procedures for advanced tumors.
principles: Organ-sparing techniques are preferred for PeIN, Ta, and select T1a lesions to preserve quality of life and sexual function.
Partial penectomy is standard for high-grade tumors (≥pT1b or ≥T2) if a functional stump can be preserved with negative margins.
Total penectomy is necessary when partial penectomy cannot achieve negative margins or for extensive invasion.
Intraoperative frozen sections are recommended to ensure negative surgical margins.
Surgical management of lymph nodes includes inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND) and pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND) based on risk stratification and nodal status.

procedures.name: Wide local excision
procedures.approach: Excision of the penile lesion with wide negative margins; may include skin grafting.
procedures.indications: PeIN, Ta, T1a lesions confined to skin with little invasion.
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence (up to 50% at 2 years if margins inadequate), wound infection, skin graft failure.

procedures.name: Glansectomy
procedures.approach: Removal of the glans penis; may be followed by split- or full-thickness skin graft for neoglans reconstruction.
procedures.indications: Distal tumors on glans or prepuce (PeIN, Ta, T1a); not recommended for pT1a unless needed for negative margins.
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence (2.6%–16.7%), urinary complications, erectile dysfunction.

procedures.name: Partial penectomy
procedures.approach: Removal of the distal penis with preservation of a functional stump; intraoperative frozen sections for margins.
procedures.indications: High-grade primary tumors (≥pT1b or ≥T2) where functional stump can be preserved.
procedures.key_complications: Positive margins requiring re-resection, urinary fistula, sexual dysfunction.

procedures.name: Total penectomy
procedures.approach: Complete removal of the penis; may include urethrostomy.
procedures.indications: When partial penectomy cannot achieve negative margins or for T4 tumors invading adjacent structures.
procedures.key_complications: Urinary diversion complications, psychological impact, sexual dysfunction.

procedures.name: Inguinal lymph node dissection (ILND)
procedures.approach: Standard or modified template; standard includes Daseler’s quadrilateral area.
procedures.indications: Staging or treatment of inguinal lymph node metastases; for intermediate/high-risk non-palpable nodes or palpable nodes.
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema, wound infection, skin necrosis, seroma.

procedures.name: Pelvic lymph node dissection (PLND)
procedures.approach: Resection of external iliac, internal iliac, and obturator lymph nodes; unilateral or bilateral.
procedures.indications: ≥2 positive inguinal nodes on ipsilateral ILND or extranodal extension; ≥4 positive inguinal nodes (bilateral PLND).

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is based on tumor stage, location, and invasion into adjacent structures. For primary tumors, invasion into corpora cavernosum may require penectomy. For lymph nodes, resectability is determined by size (<4 cm mobile nodes are resectable), fixation (fixed nodes may be borderline resectable), and presence of distant metastases. Bulky inguinal nodes (≥4 cm or fixed) may require neoadjuvant chemotherapy to downstage for surgery [PN-5, PN-7].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Pre-operative optimization includes assessment of performance status, management of comorbidities, and nutritional support. For patients with bulky nodal disease, neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy is preferred to downstage disease and improve surgical outcomes. Infection control is important, especially for overlying skin infections [PN-D, MS-13].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is used as a penile-sparing treatment for early-stage disease, as adjuvant therapy for positive surgical margins or nodal disease, and for palliation in metastatic settings. It is an alternative to surgery when organ preservation is desired or for patients who are not surgical candidates.
principles: Circumcision should always precede radiation therapy to prevent radiation-related complications like phimosis or fibrosis.
For primary penile lesions, radiation therapy can be delivered as brachytherapy with interstitial implant or external beam radiation therapy (EBRT).
Adjuvant radiation is recommended for positive margins, pN2–3 disease, or extranodal extension after lymph node dissection.
Chemoradiation is an option for unresectable or bulky disease, using concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy.

dose_frameworks.name: Brachytherapy for primary lesion
dose_frameworks.indication: T1–2, N0 tumors <4 cm for penile preservation (category 2B)

dose_frameworks.name: EBRT for primary lesion
dose_frameworks.indication: T1–2, N0 tumors or post-surgical positive margins

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant EBRT for lymph nodes
dose_frameworks.indication: Inguinal and pelvic lymph nodes after dissection

dose_frameworks.name: Boost for gross nodes or extranodal extension

approaches.name: Primary radiation/chemoradiation for penile preservation
approaches.indication: T1–2, N0 tumors <4 cm; tumors ≥4 cm or node-positive unresectable
approaches.key_trial: Crook et al. reported 10-year cause-specific survival of 84% for T1–2 lesions treated with brachytherapy [PN-C, ref 77].
approaches.toxicities: Urethral stricture, penile fibrosis, skin necrosis, erectile dysfunction

approaches.name: Adjuvant chemoradiation for node-positive disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT 45–50.4 Gy to inguinal/pelvic nodes, boost to 65–70 Gy
approaches.indication: Palpable bulky inguinal nodes, enlarged pelvic nodes, pN2–3 disease
approaches.key_trial: Limited data; extrapolated from anal and vulvar cancer studies [PN-C, ref 152-155].

approaches.name: Palliative radiation
approaches.indication: Symptomatic metastatic disease for local control

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in the neoadjuvant setting to downstage bulky nodal disease, in the adjuvant setting for high-risk features post-surgery, and as first-line or subsequent-line treatment for metastatic or recurrent penile cancer. Cisplatin-based regimens are the cornerstone, with emerging role of immunotherapy in select biomarker-defined populations.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to ILND or PLND
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Phase II study showed 50% response rate, with improved PFS and OS associated with response; preferred for ≥4 cm inguinal lymph nodes [PN-D, ref 1].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant chemotherapy following ILND or PLND
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolated from neoadjuvant data; reasonable if high-risk features and not given preoperatively [PN-D].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line systemic therapy for metastatic/recurrent disease
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Active regimen; improved survival with response [PN-D, ref 1].
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/fluorouracil, cisplatin/fluorouracil or carboplatin/fluorouracil plus pembrolizumab followed by maintenance
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: For pembrolizumab combinations, no specific biomarkers required but consideration of MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H for subsequent lines

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent-line systemic therapy for metastatic/recurrent disease
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Clinical trial or pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Pembrolizumab has tumor-agnostic indications for these biomarkers; limited data for other options [PN-D, ref 7-10].
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb for pembrolizumab eligibility

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil + Pembrolizumab followed by maintenance
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks for 6 cycles, then maintenance pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks for up to 34 cycles

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Fluorouracil + Pembrolizumab followed by maintenance
key_regimens.cycle_days: Repeat every 3 weeks for 6 cycles, then maintenance pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks for up to 34 cycles

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment is performed after neoadjuvant therapy to determine eligibility for consolidation surgery, and during systemic therapy for metastatic disease to guide further management.
response_logic: After neoadjuvant TIP chemotherapy for bulky nodal disease, imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis is repeated to assess response.
Stable or partial response to neoadjuvant therapy indicates eligibility for consolidative surgery (ILND and PLND).
Complete response may allow for surveillance, but surgery is often still considered for pathologic staging.
No response or disease progression indicates need for alternative therapies, such as clinical trials or subsequent-line systemic therapy.
In metastatic settings, response to first-line systemic therapy is assessed using imaging (e.g., CT or PET/CT) to decide on consolidation surgery or palliative care.
imaging_recommendations: Imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis with CT or MRI for initial staging and follow-up.
FDG-PET/CT may be used to assess treatment response and disease progression in patients with suspected inguinal lymph node-positive disease.
For surveillance, consider imaging of the inguinal region (CT, MRI, or ultrasound) if clinical examination is abnormal, patient is obese, or has prior inguinal surgery.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Percutaneous lymph node biopsy is recommended for diagnosis of palpable inguinal lymph nodes to confirm metastatic disease before planning treatment.
If biopsy is negative but clinical suspicion remains, excisional biopsy may be considered.
Salvage surgery (e.g., ILND) for isolated inguinal recurrence after prior treatment is possible but carries high morbidity; patient counseling is essential.
For local recurrence after penile-sparing treatment, re-biopsy and restaging are required to determine appropriate therapy based on recurrence stage.
